summary: Shapiro introduces a framework for categorizing startups into two extreme types: 'Twitter Startups' where the solution is obvious but nobody understands why anyone would want it, and 'Hoverboard Startups' where the problem is obvious but the solution seems impossibly hard. He argues that founders who don't recognize which category they fall into will botch their investor pitches. Twitter Startups should lead with traction and customer validation, while Hoverboard Startups need to demonstrate their technical breakthrough immediately. Most startups fall somewhere in between, but recognizing your type determines how you should structure your pitch.
key_points: A 'Hoverboard Startup' has a screamingly obvious problem but a prohibitively hard solution — investors only care whether you've actually cracked the technical challenge
A 'Twitter Startup' has a straightforward solution but no apparent problem — investors only care whether real users actually want it

Misidentifying your startup type leads to pitching the wrong things: Hoverboard founders waste time on market size when investors want a demo, Twitter founders waste time on tech when investors want traction
Real-world examples ground the framework: AirBnB and Instagram were Twitter Startups, Mint and EnerG2 were Hoverboard Startups, and failed examples like CueCat and Steorn show both types can fail
money_quotes: A Hoverboard Startup is one where the problem is screamingly obvious, but the solution is prohibitively hard.
A Twitter Startup is one where the solution is straightforward, but it doesn't appear to actually solve any problem.
You seriously don't care about his viral strategy or exit strategy, you just want to know if he's invented the secret of levitation.
If you're one of these and don't know it, you're going to screw up your pitch.

We also get rid of school and work affiliations, skip all the new stuff like location and checkins, and pretty much kill all the privacy settings so everything is just public by default. Won't that be AMAZING?
If you're a Hoverboard? Well, you'd better be levitating by the third slide, or your investor is going to be out of there.
key_insight: Founders must recognize whether their startup's core risk is demand (Twitter) or feasibility (Hoverboard), then structure their entire pitch around answering that single question.
